person.name: Hugh de Weston
person.userCertifiedLifeSummary:
«b»Biography«/b»
Grandson of Sir Hamo de Weston, Sir Hugh de Weston of Weston and Newton d. bef 1264 and m. Matilda, who d. c1250 and had at least two children, Hugh and John.

Gave land to the monks of St. Mary and St. Beadde of Bildewas.

Hugh conceded to Alice, former wife of Hamo de Berturton, two parts of one messuage in the vill of Weston and lands in Weston and Berturton, of which her husband died seised, until the majority of her son Thomas, the heir.

Hugh gave to William son of Hamo de Weston land in the vill of Weston held by William's father.

Witnessed a deed giving Margery, daughter of Hamo de Weston land in the vill of Berterton.

1227: Presumed to have gone on crusade.

3 Nov 1227: Fine levied on Hugh, son of John, acknowledging a virgate of land in Weston to be the right of the Prior of St. Thomas of Stafford to be held of Hugh for 6d. annually probably marks Hughs succession.

1234: Hugh de Weston and Henry de Mere (also described as Magister Henry de Weston), detained in prison at Stafford for the death of William son of John Gilles, are to be released on finding sufficient security, as it appeared on inquisition that they had been accused out of hatred and malice.

1235: The Sheriff of Shropshire and Staffordshire ordered to cause a perambulation between the lands of Roger la Zuch in Tong and the lands of Hugh de Weston in Weston.

1240: Hugh de Weston stated to hold a knight's fee in Neuton and Weston of the barony of J. FitzAlan.

1253:Escheator for Staffordshire.

1253: Hugh de Weston and Robert de Esyngton verderers sold a windfall in the hay of Geddesley to the value of 10s. by the King's order. Also 9 oaks prostrated by wind and six cartloads of branches prostrated for bote for 10s.

5 Oct 1254: One of 12 jurors for the hundred of Cuttlestone. Hugh described as lord of Weston-under-Brewed, held of the barony of Oswestry for one knight's fee. 3 hides geldable and paying 3s. to the Sheriff's aid, 3s. to the view of frank-pledge, and 12d. to the hundred. William, Rector of Weston-under-Brewed, impleaded Sir Hugh, lord of that vill, for common in his wood in the chapter of Lapley.

1256: Gives half a mark to have an assize and the Sheriff of Staffordshire is commanded to take his security.

Jul 1256: Foreman of jury of verderers and foresters of the King's Forest of Cannock ascertaining lands in the Bailiwick of Hugh de Loges.

19 Jun 1257: Gave land in Neuton to the Prior and Canons of St. Thomas the Martyr near Stafford.

1258: Paid a fine of half a gold mark to be released from sitting on an assize

nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dē ‘thigh’ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: Weston
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: habitational name from any of numerous places called Weston, from Old English west ‘west’ + tūn ‘farmstead, settlement’.

topographic name from Middle English atte west tun(e) ‘at the west farmstead, settlement’ (Old English west + tūn), or from Middle English (bi) weste(n)tune (Old English be westan tūne or be west in tūne), denoting someone who lived either to the west of a settlement or in the western part of a settlement. The latter was a common surname in Sussex.

variants.name: Wesson
meanings: English (Midlands and Kent): variant of   Weston  .
History: John Wesson came from England to Salem, MA, in 1644.

nameMeanings.name: Hugh
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: From an Old French name, Hugues, Hugo, of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from hug ‘heart, mind, spirit’. It was originally a short form of various compound names containing this element. This was borne by the aristocracy of medieval France, adopted by the Normans, and introduced by them to Britain. It was consistently one of the most popular boys' names in the Middle Ages in England, but fell out of favour in the 16th century until it was revived in the 20th century. Little Hugh of Lincoln was a child supposed in the Middle Ages to have been murdered by Jews in about 1255 , a legend responsible for several outbursts of anti-Semitism at various times. The story is referred to by Chaucer in The Prioress's Tale. He is not to be confused with St Hugh of Lincoln ( 1140–1200 ), bishop of Lincoln ( 1186–1200 ), who was noted for his charity and good works, his piety, and his defence of the Church against the State. Since the later Middle Ages Hugh has also been used as an Anglicized form of the Gaelic name Aodh .

historicEvents.title: Magna Carta
historicEvents.description: King John of England agreed to a charter of rights called the Magna Carta. The document was originally drafted by the Archbishop of Canterbury to establish peace between King John and a group of rebel barons. The Magna Carta was completed in 1215 and is now considered to be one of the greatest constitutional documents of all time.

historicEvents.title: France Declares War on England
historicEvents.description: France declared war on England as Louis VIII of France attacked Poitou and northern Gascony.

historicEvents.title: 100 Years of Conflict Between England and France Ends
historicEvents.description: Treaty of Paris was signed on December 4, 1259, which ended 100 years of conflict between England and France.

en.lifeSummary: When Hugh de Weston was born in 1195, in Weston, Staffordshire, England, his father, John De Weston, Magister, was 26 and his mother, Isolde de Newton, was 22. He married Matilda Brereton in 1226, in Weston, Staffordshire, England, United Kingdom. They were the parents of at least 2 sons. He died after 1258, in Weston, Staffordshire, England, United Kingdom.
